coredata.dc:title: Polymerizable aggregation-induced emission dye for preparation of cross-linkable fluorescent nanoprobes with ultra-low critical micelle concentrations
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Long
Liu, Meiying
Mao, Liucheng
Zhang, Xiqi
Xu, Dazhuang
Wan, Qing
Huang, Qiang
Shi, Yingge
Deng, Fengjie
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dyes based fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) have achieved significant progress in various biomedical applications. In this work, we developed a covalent strategy to prepare biocompatible AIE-active dyes based cross-linked copolymers (MPC-POSS-PhE) via controllable reversible addition fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization using zwitterionic 2-methacryloyloxyethyl phosphorylcholine (MPC), polymerizable AIE dye (named as PhE) and 8-vinyl polyoctahedral silsesquioxanes (POSS) as monomers. Due to the existence of hydrophilic MPC and hydrophobic PhE, the resultant copolymers will self-assemble into core-shell nanoparticles in aqueous solution with ultra-low critical micelle concentration (CMC). This could effectively overcome the drawbacks of non-crosslinked micelles and show more attractive properties and better performance for biomedical applications. Furthermore, the characterization results and biological assays demonstrated that the final MPC-POSS-PhE FONs show stable aqueous stability, uniform size and morphology, high water dispersity, desirable optical properties and low cytotoxicity. These remarkable properties make the resultant AIE-active nanoprobes great potential for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Cross-linkable
Critical micelle concentrations
Biological imaging
Low cytotoxicity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066), the National 973 Project (Nos. 2011CB935700).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FT-IR spectra of PhE and MPC-POSS-PhE.
XPS wide scan of MPC-POSS-PhE. Survey scans the spectral region from 0 to 1200eV.
The high resolution XPS of MPC-POSS-PhE. (A) the C1s region, (B) the O1s region, (C) the Si2p region, (D) the N1s region, (E) the P2p region, and the S2p region.

UV–Vis spectrum of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs. Three adsorption peaks located at 250, 338 and 433nm can be found. The inset optical image is the water suspension of MPC-POSS-PhE. These characteristic adsorption peaks and great water dispersity of MPC-POSS-PhE suggested that PhE has successfully polymerized with MPC.

A) The fluorescence spectra of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs dispersed in aqueous solution (λex =451nm, λem =585nm). B) The representative fluorescent curves of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs dispersed in aqueous solution before and after UV lamp irradiation. The results demonstrated the great photostability of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs.
Intensity of the aggregate emission vs. the logarithm of the concentration ofMPC-POSS-PhE FONs, indicating that the CMC value is as low as 0.007mgmL−1.
The TEM image of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs.
Cell viability of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs based on the CCK-8 assay. The HeLa cells were incubated with 10–120μgmL−1 MPC-POSS-PhE FONs for 12h and 24h.

The CLSM images of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs. The cell images were obtained from CLSM using 458nm laser excitation. HeLa cells were incubated with 30μgmL−1 of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs for 3h. (A) Bright field, (B) fluorescent image, (C) merged image of A and B.
Schematic showing the preparation of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs.

head.ce:title: Polymerizable aggregation-induced emission dye for preparation of cross-linkable fluorescent nanoprobes with ultra-low critical micelle concentrations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dyes based fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) have achieved significant progress in various biomedical applications. In this work, we developed a covalent strategy to prepare biocompatible AIE-active dyes based cross-linked copolymers (MPC-POSS-PhE) via controllable reversible addition fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization using zwitterionic 2-methacryloyloxyethyl phosphorylcholine (MPC), polymerizable AIE dye (named as PhE) and 8-vinyl polyoctahedral silsesquioxanes (POSS) as monomers. Due to the existence of hydrophilic MPC and hydrophobic PhE, the resultant copolymers will self-assemble into core-shell nanoparticles in aqueous solution with ultra-low critical micelle concentration (CMC). This could effectively overcome the drawbacks of non-crosslinked micelles and show more attractive properties and better performance for biomedical applications. Furthermore, the characterization results and biological assays demonstrated that the final MPC-POSS-PhE FONs show stable aqueous stability, uniform size and morphology, high water dispersity, desirable optical properties and low cytotoxicity. These remarkable properties make the resultant AIE-active nanoprobes great potential for biomedical applications.

Cross-linked fluorescent organic nanoparticles with aggregation-induced emission were fabricated through reversible addition fragmentation chain transfer polymerization using 8-vinyl polyoctahedral silsesquioxanes as the linkage and utilized for biological imaging.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid advances in functional materials, multifunctional nanoparticles such as fluorescent nanoparticles have revealed unparalleled potential in the field of bioimaging and biosensors [1–3]. Fluorescent nanoparticles have shown unique advantages in term of high quantum yield, superior photo-bleaching resistance, large stokes shifts, excellent biocompatibility and good cellular-internalization efficiency, which can be divided into fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles (FINs) and fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) [4–14]. Among FINs, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have achieved significant progress in the biological applications over the past two decades due to their high brightness, large stokes shift and better photostability [15]. More importantly, the more feasible surface modification of QDs expand their applications from electronic materials science to biological applications [16]. However, it is worth mentioning that the inherent toxicity of QDs has greatly impaired their biomedical applications because most QDs would inevitably release free heavy-metal ions [17]. Furthermore, apart from inorganic fluorescent materials, fluorophores as a kind of organic fluorescent materials have been widely used in biomedical fields [18]. Among them, fluorescent proteins that possess unparalleled biocompatibility and very high quantum yield seemed to be the best candidates for biomedical applications [19]. Unfortunately, fluorescent proteins are easy degraded by enzyme in the organism and show poor photostability, thereby limiting their cell imaging applications because of short lifetime. Besides, compared with inorganic QDs and fluorescent proteins, organic fluorophores such as rhodamine not only exhibit high fluorescent quantum yield and remarkable photostability, but also can be easy functionalized with other components for various biomedical applications. Nevertheless, such conventional organic fluorophores usually suffer from aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) when their concentrations reach a certain level. The fluorophores are usually piled up together when they are encapsulated into nanoparticles, so the emission is quenched due to strong intermolecular π-π stacking interaction [20]. In this case, preparation of FONs without ACQ effect is highly demanded.

Fortunately, in contrast with the conventional fluorophores, a type of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dyes is highly emission in the aggregate state or solid state due to the mechanism of restriction of intramolecular motions (RIM) [21,22]. Although AIE dyes show unique fluorescent property, their intrinsic hydrophobicity predestinate that they could not be directly applied in biomedical fields [23–29]. Hence, incorporation of AIE dyes into amphiphilic polymers to achieve high aqueous dispersibility and high luminescence intensity has been developed in recent years [30–44]. Although many non-covalent and covalent methods have been developed previously, most of these self-assemblies also suffer from other problems such as instability in diluted solution. In other word, these micelles exhibited high critical micelle concentration (CMC) in aqueous solution. Therefore, the preparation of cross-linked AIE-active micelles with ultra-low critical micelle concentration (CMC) is still highly demanded for cell imaging applications.

In this contribution, a covalent strategy to fabricate biocompatible AIE-active cross-linked copolymers (MPC-POSS-PhE) was developed by controllable reversible addition fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization for the first time. On the one hand, the zwitterionic polymers Poly (2-methacryloyloxyethyl phosphorylcholine) (MPC) possess super-hydrophilicity and excellent biocompatibility, which have much better performance in cellular uptake and cell imaging. On the other hand, the commercially available octavinyl-T8-silsesquioxane (8-vinyl POSS) with eight polymerizable double bonds can be introduced as a cross-linked center. As shown in Scheme 1 , we flexibly mixed up polymerizable AIE dyes (PhE), 8-vinyl POSS and MPC in a bottle to prepare MPC-POSS-PhE copolymer by RAFT polymerization. Thanks to existence of the cross-linked center, thus obtained MPC-POSS-PhE copolymers are prone to self-assemble into stable AIE dyes based FONs with ultra-low CMC in aqueous solution. Subsequently, a series of characterizations proved that the resulted MPC-POSS-PhE FONs revealed ultra-low CMC, high water dispersity, outstanding photostability resistance and excellent biocompatibility, which greatly promoted its applications for cell imaging.

All reagents and solvents were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. 2-Methacryloyloxy ethyl phosphorylcholine (MPC), octavinyl-T8-silsesquioxane (8-vinyl POSS) and azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were provided from Aladdin. Anhydrous N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was supplied by Heowns (Tianjin, China). Ultra-pure water was used in the experiments. The chain transfer agent (CTA) of 4-cyano-4-(ethylthiocarbonothioylthio) pentanoic acid were prepared by the literature methods [45].

1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were collected at room temperature on a Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer with CDCl3 and d6-DMSO as the solvent. The peak positions were calibrated by using TMS as an internal standard. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in a transmission mode on a Nicolet5700 spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet Corporation). Typically, 8 scans at a resolution of 1cm−1 were accumulated to obtain one spectrum. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on the fluorescence spectrophotometer (FSP, model: C11367–11) purchased from Hamamatsu (Japanese). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was captured with a Hitachi 7650B instrument operated at 80kV. The sample for TEM measurements was prepared by dropping the solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was supplied from ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific). The size and zeta potential of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs in aqueous solution was measured by size and potential measuring instrument (model: Zetasizer nano zs90).

The polymerizable AIE dye (PhE) is shown in Scheme 1 and its synthetic routes can be found in our previous publications [29]. The amphiphilic triblock copolymers (MPC-POSS-PhE) are fabricated via RAFT polymerization. For the synthesis of the MPC-POSS-PhE copolymer, 0.012g of 8-vinyl POSS (0.02mmol), 0.065g of PhE (0.10mmol), 0.275g of MPC (1.00mmol), 5mg of CTA (0.02mmol), 5mg of the initiator AIBN (0.03mmol) and 10mL of anhydrous DMF were mixed in Schlenk tube. Then the solution was flushed with N2 for 30min and the Schlenk tube was placed in an oil bath and heated up carefully to 80°C. The mixture was stirred for 10h at 80°C under N2 atmosphere. After the reaction was completed, the mixture was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and dialyzed against tap water for 24h then ethanol for 6h using 3500DaMW cutoff dialysis bag. Finally, the resulting solution in dialysis bag was carried out by freeze-drying to obtain the products.

In order to evaluate the effect of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs on living cells, the cell viability of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs with strong yellow fluorescence on HeLa and A549 cells was evaluated by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay [46–49]. The clear procedure could be demonstrated by follows, cells were put into 96-well microplates at a density of 5×104 cells mL−1 in 160μL of respective media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). After 24h of cell attachment, the cells were incubated with different concentrations of FONs (10–120μgmL−1) for 12h and 24h. Then nanoparticles were removed and cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) three times. 10μL of CCK-8 dye and 100μL of Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) were added to each well and incubated for 2h at room temperature. Afterward, plates were analyzed using a microplate reader (VictorШ, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of formazan dye absorbance were carried out at 450nm, with the reference wavelength at 620nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of CCK-8 dye was compared to controls, which represented 100% CCK-8 reduction. Three replicate wells were used per microplate, and the experiment was operated for three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of untreated controls. Results are presented as mean±standard deviation (SD).

HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS, 2mM glutamine, 100UmL−1 penicillin, and 100μgmL−1 of streptomycin. Cell culture was controlled at 37°C in a similar humidified condition of 95% air and 5% CO2 in culture medium. Culture medium should be updated every three days for maintaining the exponential growth of the cells. Before treatment, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1×105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with MPC-POSS-PhE FONs at a final concentration of 30μgmL−1 for 3h at 37°C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the MPC-POSS-PhE FONs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature. Cell images were obtained using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zeiss 710 3-channel (Zeiss, Germany) with the excitation wavelength of 458nm.

To confirm the successful synthesis of MPC-POSS-PhE copolymer, 1H NMR spectra of PhE and MPC-POSS-PhE were recorded for comparison. As shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. 1 , the characteristic chemical shifts of double bonds of PhE located at 5.3 and 5.8ppm disappeared in MPC-POSS-PhE, demonstrating the successful polymerization of PhE. Although two small peaks located at 5.6 and 6.0ppm appeared in MPC-POSS-PhE, these peaks should be attributed to double bonds from 8-vinyl POSS or MPC because many double bonds from 8-vinyl POSS cannot entirely involved in the polymerization reaction. The chemical shift of 1.2ppm derived from the methylene groups, which can be observed in the both PhE and MPC-POSS-PhE. Besides, the chemical shift of 3.1 and 3.3ppm generated from the methylene groups of MPC can be identified in the MPC-POSS-PhE. More importantly, as shown in inset image of Fig. 1, the peaks of aromatic proton were obviously broadened in the range of 6.8 to 8.0ppm from the as-prepared MPC-POSS-PhE, which forcefully proved the successful polymerization of PhE. All above evidence suggested MPC-POSS-PhE copolymers were successfully prepared via RAFT polymerization with POSS as the cross-linkage.

The successful preparation of MPC-POSS-PhE copolymers was further confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 , a series of peaks located between 1400 and 1600cm−1, which can be obviously observed in the sample of PhE and can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the polycyclic aromatic ring. Furthermore, the characteristic peak located at 2211cm−1 was the stretching vibration of CN bond and other two characteristic peaks of 2848 and 2920cm−1 were the stretching vibration of CH2 groups. These evidences confirmed that the polymerizable AIE dye PhE had been successfully synthesized. Compared with the characteristic peaks of PhE, the same peaks at 2924 and 2986cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of CH2 groups could be observed in the as-prepared PEG-POSS-PhE. Moreover, the CC bond at 1630cm−1 belong to PhE had almost disappeared, indicating the successful incorporation of PhE into the copolymer. More importantly, these new evident peaks such as 1650cm−1 (stretching vibration of CO), 1070cm−1 (stretching vibration of CO) and 950cm−1 (stretching vibration of SiO), which emerged in the spectrum of PEG-POSS-PhE, further confirming that successful incorporation of polymerizable AIE dye into the copolymers. Combined 1H NMR with FT-IR spectra, it can clearly draw a conclusion that MPC-POSS-PhE copolymers were successfully fabricated.

The optical properties of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs in water are used to assess the value of its biomedical applications. The absorption and photoluminescence spectra of an aqueous dispersion of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 , three adsorption peaks cantered at 250, 338 and 433nm can be found in the UV–Vis spectrum of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs. The adsorption peak at 250nm can be attributed to the π→π* transition of aromatic rings, which were conjugated with double bonds. The adsorption peak at 338nm can be attributed to the red shift of conjugated system, which conjugated with –CN group. Moreover, the adsorption peak at 433nm can be assigned to the adsorption of phenothiazine. Therefore, the UV–Vis spectrum could also indicate that the AIE dye PhE has incorporated in final MPC-POSS-PhE copolymers. The water suspension of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs was displayed in the inset image of Fig. 5. It can be seen that MPC-POSS-PhE can be well dispersed in pure water solution and showed uniform suspension with yellow color. The logo “Nanchang University” was also clearly distinguished as the background. The excellent dispersity of MPC-POSS-PhE suspension also suggested that the hydrophobic AIE dye PhE has successfully polymerized with the hydrophilic MPC.

The fluorescent spectra of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs were shown in Fig. 6 . It can be seen that a sharp emission peak at 585nm with full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 68nm can be found in the emission spectrum of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs. Using 585nm as the emission peak, the excitation spectrum of was determined. It can be seen that broad excitation region with two peaks can be found. The maximum excitation peak is located at 451nm. The above results suggested that MPC-POSS-PhE FONs can be excited using different lasers when they were used for biological imaging. Moreover, the excitation and emission spectra of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs display a large stokes shift of 134nm in aqueous solution, which possess unique advantages such as high-performance optical imaging and enhancing the signal fidelity for cell imaging applications. The inset of Fig. 6A shows the optical image of MPC-POSS-PhE water suspension under UV lamp at 365nm. Intensive and uniform yellow fluorescence can be observed. This is well consistent with the fluorescent spectra results. Furthermore, photostability is an essential and important parameter that can be used to evaluate the efficiency of fluorescent biological probe for biomedical applications. As shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescent intensity of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs dispersed in aqueous solution was measured before and after UV lamp irradiation. It can be seen that the maximum fluorescent intensity is 846a.u. before UV lamp irradiation. After MPC-POSS-PhE FONs were irradiated using UV lamp for 60min, the maximum fluorescent intensity down to 798a.u. There was almost no change in the fluorescent intensity, suggestive of great photostability of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs. On the other hand, the photostability of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs in the present of solution with different ionic strength and temperature was also evaluated. It has been demonstrated that the ionic strength and temperature showed little influence on the photostability of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs (Figs. S2 and S3). All of the above results demonstrated MPC-POSS-PhE FONs possess excellent optical properties for biomedical applications.

The CMC of polymeric assemblies is very important for their practical biomedical applications especially for their utilization in diluted environment. It is well known that the incorporation of cross-linkages is a useful and effective method to enhance the solution stability of self-assemble polymers. To evaluate the effect of POSS cross-linkage on the solution stability of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs, the CMC value of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs was determined using fluorescent spectra. As shown in Fig. 7 , the intensity of the aggregate emission vs. the logarithm of the concentration of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs was carried out to determine the CMC via the tangent method. Obviously, when the concentration of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs is higher than the CMC, the fluorescent intensity increased abruptly. However, at a concentration of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs below the CMC, the intensity of the aggregate emission is very low. This phenomenon should be attributed to the luminous mechanism of AIE dyes. Based on our measurement, we could calculate that the CMC value of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs is as low as 0.007mgmL−1. In other word, the MPC-POSS-PhE FONs can be kept stable when the final concentration of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs is above 7μgmL−1. These superiority properties including high water dispersity, excellent optical properties and ultra-low CMC, endow MPC-POSS-PhE FONs great potential for biomedical applications.

The resulting amphiphilic triblock copolymers (MPC-POSS-PhE) were prone to self-assemble into stable nanoparticles in aqueous solution. Besides, introducing 8-vinyl POSS as the cross-linkage, the obtained copolymers could form core-shell structure in which the AIE dye (PhE) aggregates confined in the hydrophobic cores and the hydrophilic ends (MPC) are expanded to water forming a uniform shell. Therefore, in order to confirm the above hypothesis, the size and morphology of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs were investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 8 , a lot of uniform spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 200nm could be clearly seen. More importantly, MPC-POSS-PhE FONs display excellent dispersion in aqueous solution, which can be identified from the TEM image. Furthermore, the size distribution of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs in aqueous solution was determined by DLS, yielding the size distribution as 250±89nm (Fig. S4). Zeta potential analysis of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs was further carried out, and MPC-POSS-PhE FONs were negatively charged (−6.5mV), which was ascribed to the weak intermolecular interaction of the surface MPC or the deprotonization of carboxyl groups from CTA in neutral conditions. Consequently, the as-prepared MPC-POSS-PhE FONs possess uniform spherical morphology and high water dispersibility, which is anticipated to be great potential for biomedical applications.

In order to confirm the potential biomedical applications of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs, their effect on cells should be first evaluated. In this work, the cell viability based on CCK-8 assay were determined and used to give the preliminary biocompatibility results of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs. As shown in Fig. 9 , no cell viability values decrease was observed when cells were incubated with 10–120μgmL−1 of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs for 12h and 24h, respectively. It is worth mentioning that the cell viability is still greater than 95% even when the concentrations of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs were as high as 120μgmL−1. The cell viability of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs towards A549 cells was also determined using CCK-8 assay. Well consistent with the results from HeLa cells. MPC-POSS-PhE FONs also displayed low cytotoxicity towards A549 cells. All the cell viability values are greater than 95% after 24h incubation (Fig. S5). Hence, these results confirmed that neglectable cytotoxicity of the as-prepared MPC-POSS-PhE FONs should be very vital for biomedical applications.

On the premise of neglectable cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, the in vitro cellular uptake and cell imaging of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs were evaluated by CLSM observation. When irradiated with a 458nm laser, the CLSM imaging of cells only incubated with 30μgmL−1 of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs for 3h were shown in Fig. 10 . First of all, cells still kept their normal morphology, which can be observed from bright field image displayed in Fig. 10A. The above result further confirmed that MPC-POSS-PhE FONs possess good biocompatibility. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 10B, very abundant and bright fluorescence can be clearly detected when cells were irradiated with 458nm laser. However, there are lots of dark areas were surrounded by the areas of fluorescent signals. Finally, from the merged image in Fig. 10C, we can observe that the areas with fluorescent signals were overlapped with the location of cells. Owing to non-specific targeting molecules for cell targeting, these results suggested that MPC-POSS-PhE FONs can be internalized by cells through the endocytosis pathway. Moreover, through combination of the above three CLSM images, we can draw the conclusion that the black areas in the cells should be the locations of cell nuclei and MPC-POSS-PhE FONs are mainly distributed in the cytoplasm. Therefore, benefit by intense fluorescence, great water dispersity and excellent biocompatibility, all above results indicated that MPC-POSS-PhE FONs should become promising candidates for biomedical applications.

In summary, AIE dyes based biocompatible cross-linked copolymers (MPC-POSS-PhE) were synthesized through RAFT polymerization of the polymerizable AIE-active PhE, zwitterionic MPC and commercially available 8-vinyl POSS. The structure was fully characterized by 1H NMR and FT-IR spectra. Such MPC-POSS-PhE copolymers are prone to self-assemble into stable core–shell nanoparticles in aqueous solution because the hydrophobic PhE is clustered as the core and hydrophilic MPC are exposed to water forming a uniform shell. Furthermore, through introducing 8-vinyl POSS as the cross-linkage, the obtained copolymers could form stable structure and self-assemble into more stable nanoparticles in aqueous solution with ultra-low CMC (0.007mgmL−1). The excellent properties of MPC-POSS-PhE FONs, including great water dispersity, high brightness, remarkable photostability and large stokes shifts, further indicating their potential application in cell imaging. More importantly, through the CCK-8 assay and CLSM observation, MPC-POSS-PhE FONs show excellent biocompatibility and intense fluorescence. All above experimental data could conclude that MPC-POSS-PhE FONs possess better performance for biomedical applications as compared with the non-cross-linked ones. Moreover, multifunctional AIE-active dye based polymeric systems could also be fabricated via incorporation of drug binding groups, targeting agents and other imaging components.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066), the National 973 Project (Nos. 2011CB935700).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.03.122.
